title: Gate
summary: Silences the signal when it drops below the threshold — the opposite of a compressor. Used to remove noise from reverb tails, create rhythmic chop effects via sidechain triggering, or clean up sustained sounds.
steps: Add Gate to the track you want to gate
Threshold: the level below which the gate closes (silences the signal). Set it just above the noise floor to gate silently
Attack: how quickly the gate opens when the signal exceeds threshold. Fast (0.5ms) = snappy gate open. Slow (20ms) = gradual fade-in
Hold: minimum time the gate stays open after the signal exceeds threshold. Useful for notes that briefly dip below threshold mid-sustain
Release: how quickly the gate closes after the signal drops below threshold. Fast = abrupt chop. Slow = gradual fade-out
For rhythmic gating: enable Sidechain, set Audio From to a rhythmic source track. The gate opens and closes with that source's amplitude — creates a rhythmic chop effect
tips: Gated reverb (famous 1980s drum sound): add a very long Reverb to a snare, then Gate after the reverb. Set short Hold — the reverb blooms then gets chopped off abruptly
For noise removal: set Threshold just above the hiss/noise floor. The gate closes when the signal is quiet (just noise) and opens when the actual content plays
Rhythmic gating via sidechain: a pad gated from a kick pattern creates a chopped pad that pulses with the kick rhythm — a different effect from sidechain compression (which ducks, not chops)
Gate vs Compressor: Compressor reduces volume above threshold. Gate silences below threshold. They work on opposite sides of the threshold
practice_hint: In a session with a chord pad and reverb, try adding Gate after the reverb send's return track. Set Threshold to -30dB, Hold 200ms. Hear how the reverb tail is cut off rather than decaying naturally.
ableton_version_note: Available in all editions of Live 9+.
